figures.gloss: The whole universe shaped like a vast man standing arms-akimbo — the hells stacked in his legs, the middle world at his waist, the heavens in his chest, and the liberated souls a crescent at the crown of his head. It was never made and will never end.
figures.facet: The precious counter-shard — a cosmos with NO creator: the Loka is eternal, uncaused, self-existent, neither born from a sky-father nor hatched from an egg nor spoken into being. Where every other tradition seats a maker at the centre of its axis, Jainism puts only impersonal substance and law, and dares the universe to need no god at all.
figures.provenance: Tattvārtha Sūtra (Umāsvāti, c. 2nd–5th c. CE) and the Lokaprakāśa cosmographies; the anthropomorphic loka-puruṣa is a later iconographic systematisation of the older three-world scheme.

figures.gloss: Time as an ever-turning wheel of twelve spokes — six ages descending from a golden ease into a leaden misery (avasarpiṇī), then six ascending back up (utsarpiṇī) — grinding round forever without beginning or final end.
figures.facet: Decline and renewal with no destroyer and no judgment: the ages worsen and mend by the wheel's own turning, not by a god's wrath or a saviour's return. Even the Tīrthaṅkaras can appear only in certain spokes — the cosmic order outranks every teacher who arises within it.
figures.provenance: Śvetāmbara and Digambara cosmologies; the avasarpiṇī/utsarpiṇī scheme is canonical across the Tattvārtha and Āgama traditions.

figures.gloss: The ford-makers — the recurring series of fully liberated teachers who, in each descending half-cycle, build the 'ford' (tīrtha) across the river of rebirth by re-founding the four-fold community and re-teaching the eternal law. Twenty-four arise in each cycle, the same office filled again and again.
figures.facet: One pattern, twenty-four faces, no god among them: the Tīrthaṅkaras are not avatars of a deity nor incarnations of one being but separate souls who each independently reached the same summit. The 'one-through-many' here is not one essence refracted but one ATTAINMENT repeated — a democracy of liberation, not an emanation.
figures.provenance: Listed in the Kalpa Sūtra and Tattvārtha tradition; Ṛṣabha first, Pārśva (23rd) and Mahāvīra (24th) historically attested c. 8th and 6th–5th c. BCE.

figures.gloss: The first Tīrthaṅkara of this cycle — the Bull-lord who, when the wish-granting trees of the golden age failed, taught humankind agriculture, fire, crafts, writing, law and kingship before renouncing it all to win liberation.
figures.facet: The benefactor who then walks away: Ṛṣabha gives civilisation not as a stolen gift nor a divine decree but as a stage to be outgrown — the culture-hero whose final teaching is that the arts he founded are themselves a bondage to renounce.
figures.provenance: Kalpa Sūtra and Ādi Purāṇa (Jinasena, 9th c. CE); his teaching of the 'six occupations' is the Jain civilisational origin-myth.

figures.gloss: The twenty-fourth and last Tīrthaṅkara of this descending age — the historical Vardhamāna, contemporary of the Buddha, who through extreme asceticism conquered all passion (jina, 'victor') and re-proclaimed the eternal teaching of non-violence and the many-sidedness of truth.
figures.facet: A founder who founds nothing new: Mahāvīra insists he only re-teaches what Pārśva and the twenty-two before him taught and the next cycle's Tīrthaṅkaras will teach again — authority by re-discovery of an eternal law, never by revelation or invention.
figures.provenance: Ācārāṅga and Kalpa Sūtra; the only Tīrthaṅkara with firm historical footing alongside Pārśva (c. 599–527 BCE by traditional dating).

figures.gloss: The countless eternal souls — each uncreated, each intrinsically all-knowing and blissful, each weighed down only by the karmic matter (ajīva) clinging to it. Every living thing, down to a drop of water or a flame, houses a jīva straining toward release.
figures.facet: Infinite souls, no over-soul: against the Vedic Ātman dissolving into one Brahman, Jainism holds the souls eternally PLURAL and distinct even in liberation. Reality is the standing tension of two substances, soul and non-soul — a dualism of spirit and matter rather than good versus evil.
figures.provenance: Tattvārtha Sūtra; the jīva/ajīva division and karmic-matter doctrine are foundational Jain metaphysics, explicitly counter-Upaniṣadic.

figures.gloss: The supreme vow — to injure no living thing in thought, word or deed, since every creature down to the unseen one-sensed beings of earth, water, fire and air bears a soul as worthy as one's own. The monk sweeps his path and strains his water; the lay-person foreswears the harming trades.
figures.facet: Law kept by restraint, not sacrifice: where neighbouring cults bound the cosmos with blood-offering, Jainism binds it by withholding the knife entirely. The right-order of the world is upheld by what one refuses to do — non-action as the highest rite.
figures.provenance: Ācārāṅga Sūtra; ahiṃsā as the first and governing vow (mahāvrata) is the ethical core of the whole tradition.

figures.gloss: The doctrine that reality has infinite aspects and no single statement captures it whole — every truth is partial, asserted only 'from a standpoint' (syādvāda), so the blind men and the elephant are all correct and all incomplete.
figures.facet: Complementarity raised to an epistemology: where Yin-Yang or Qian-Kun balance two cosmic powers, anekāntavāda multiplies the standpoints to infinity and makes humility itself the law of knowing — the refusal of any final word as a spiritual discipline.
figures.provenance: Systematised by Kundakunda and Siddhasena Divākara (c. 5th c. CE); syādvāda (the 'sevenfold predication') is its logical form.

figures.gloss: The crescent at the very summit of the cosmic man, just below the roof of the universe, where every liberated soul rises and rests forever — omniscient, bodiless, passionless, each keeping its own individuality while sharing one identical state of bliss.
figures.facet: Liberation without merger and without a god to greet it: the freed soul does not join a deity or dissolve into an absolute — it simply rises to the ceiling of the world and abides, self-sufficient. The 'one-through-many' completed as countless equals, not as a return to the One.
figures.provenance: Tattvārtha Sūtra and Jain cosmography; the soul's upward motion to the siddha-śilā at liberation is canonical.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision the soul as a luminous entity trapped in physical cycles, striving for ascent and liberation by shedding its material bindings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Total vulnerability and the refusal of harm as the absolute prerequisite for spiritual purification, enacted physically before the assembly.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A class of completely perfected beings who have exhausted all their karma and permanently exited the cycle of reincarnation, though here they actively manage Earth.
GOLD SEAM: A succession of perfectly enlightened 'ford-makers' who renew the eternal religion in each cosmic cycle.
GOLD SEAM: Both view time as moving in vast, cyclical ages of spiritual renewal, though the Bahá'í model emphasizes an upward evolutionary spiral rather than strict endless repetition.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ultimate goal of ancient Indian shramana movements: a human who has completely burned away karmic defilements to achieve irreversible personal liberation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision an inherently pure, enlightened core obscured by accumulated layers of karmic dirt that must be cleared to reveal its natural omniscience.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both treat single belief systems as fundamentally incomplete, asserting that truth is contextual and multiple perspectives must be actively utilized.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A mechanistic, inevitable turning of the cosmic epochs that reshapes the fundamental nature of the physical and moral universe.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate human paragon who achieves cosmic perfection entirely through self-cultivation and moral rigor, without relying on divine grace, serving as a beacon for others.
GOLD SEAM: A foundational ethical stance of profound care for other living beings, though Ren is specifically relational and anthropocentric rather than ascetic.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Though approaching from different angles, both rigorously reject singular dogmatic revelation in favor of a broader, philosophical apprehension of truth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conceptualization of cosmic time as a massive, rotating wheel divided into distinct, determining spokes or eras.
GOLD SEAM: The eternal, indestructible soul that transmigrates endlessly through physical vessels.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature a lineage of enlightened pathmakers who have crossed the ocean of illusion and left a trail for others to follow.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit an eternal, uncreated cosmos governed by strict, impersonal material laws without the need for a supreme creator god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Addresses the cyclical, mechanical degeneration of the cosmos over vast eons, intervening at the absolute lowest point of spiritual decline.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both view the cosmos as a rigidly structured, multi-tiered architecture that functions as a massive, impersonal machine bound by descending laws.
GOLD SEAM: Perfection is achieved not through divine grace, but by the laborious, self-directed chipping away of accumulated karmic or material superfluities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize time not as a linear progression toward eschatology, but as an endlessly revolving wheel that governs the physical cycles of the cosmos.
GOLD SEAM: Liberated, discarnate masters existing at the apex of the spiritual hierarchy, providing an ultimate ideal and psychic authority rather than bodily presence.
GOLD SEAM: Ascetic figures fundamentally linked to the bull, defined by radical physical stillness and inner heat rather than external combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel regarding the soul as an animating life essence that must be carefully managed to maintain purity and health.
The same uncreated cosmos seen in space and in time: the eternal Loka endures while the Wheel of Time turns within it — two faces of one beginningless, makerless universe.
The ford-makers are scheduled BY the wheel — twenty-four arise per descending half-cycle and none can appear outside their appointed spokes; the office is a function of cyclic time itself.
The first ford-maker of this cycle — Ṛṣabha opens the series of twenty-four, the one-through-many begun.
The last ford-maker of this cycle — Mahāvīra closes the series Ṛṣabha opened, re-teaching what all twenty-four eternally teach.
Because every jīva is an eternal soul as precious as one's own, harming any is the gravest karmic bondage — ahiṃsā is the direct ethical consequence of the soul-doctrine.
The same soul before and after: the jīva burdened with karmic matter is the soul that, cleansed, rises to the Siddha-loka and abides forever distinct.

Sibling Indic systematisations of beginningless cyclic time — both teach worlds rising and falling by impersonal law with no creator to start the round; sister-śramaṇa traditions of the same Gangetic ferment.
Both Indic cosmologies set the lived world inside vast recurring world-ages; the Vedic yuga-cycle and the Jain kāla-cakra are parallel readings of declining and renewing time.
Two śramaṇa answers from the same age and region: both displace Vedic blood-sacrifice and a creator-god, Jainism by the vow of non-harm, Buddhism by no-self — kindred refusals, not derivations.
The sharpest fault-line between the śramaṇa siblings: Jainism makes the eternal plural soul the bedrock of reality; Buddhism denies any abiding self at all. Same problem, opposite verdict.

Against the Upaniṣadic merger of Ātman into one Brahman, Jainism insists souls stay eternally MANY and distinct even when freed — a deliberate doctrinal counter to Vedic non-dualism it grew up beside.

Both split reality into conscious spirit and unconscious matter — jīva/ajīva mirrors puruṣa/prakṛti as Indic spirit-matter dualisms, though Jainism multiplies the souls where Sāṃkhya keeps puruṣas plural too.

Jainism keeps the Indic three-world axis and the world-mountain but evicts the gods from its peak — where Meru is crowned by Brahmā's heaven, the Loka's summit holds only liberated souls and no creator anywhere.
Both śramaṇa cosmographies inherit the Meru-centred vertical world and then hollow out its theism — a makerless axis-mundi in two neighbouring traditions.
The counter-shard at its sharpest: where Vedānta makes the cosmos the play of one absolute, Jainism denies any ground-of-being — only eternal substances and law, no One behind the many.
Both arrange the cosmos vertically as worlds stacked on an axis — hells below, lived world at the middle, blessed at the top — though the Loka is a man's body where Yggdrasil is a tree.

The cosmic-man shape echoes the Vedic Puruṣa whose body becomes the world — but the Jain Loka is no one's body and is never sacrificed; the form is borrowed, the primordial-sacrifice pointedly absent.

Both bring fire and the civilising arts to a helpless humankind — but Ṛṣabha gives freely as a king and then renounces the gift, where Prometheus steals it and is punished. The culture-hero as renouncer, not rebel.
Both are the founder-king of the arts who finally departs the kingdom he taught — agriculture, writing and law given, then the throne abandoned for a higher liberation.

The same śramaṇa victory under one image: Mahāvīra the 'victor' (jina) conquers all passion by asceticism just as the Buddha sees through Māra's armies — contemporaries of one age and region, kindred conquests of desire, not one borrowed from the other.
Both crown non-action: ahiṃsā upholds the world by the knife withheld as the Dao's wu-wei upholds it by the deed unforced — right order kept by restraint rather than assertion.

Both refuse the single absolute claim — Yin-Yang by pairing every truth with its complement, anekāntavāda by multiplying standpoints to infinity; complementarity as two poles versus complementarity as countless.
Where Advaita resolves all standpoints into one undivided truth, anekāntavāda holds that no standpoint is ever final — pluralism of perspective set against non-dual unity.

Liberation as the great divider: the Jain soul rises to the world's ceiling and abides forever ITSELF, while the Vedic ātman is freed by dissolving INTO Brahman — release as persistence versus release as merger.
Both deny a creator-ground, yet the freed Jain jīva remains an eternal individual where the Buddhist nirvāṇa extinguishes the very notion of an abiding self — kindred denials, opposite destinies.
The deepest divergence in the mirror: against the sky-father at the head of nearly every pantheon, the Loka enthrones no one — an uncreated, fatherless cosmos that needs no god to hold it up.

Both are states of ultimate liberation — but the World to Come is a communal resurrection in history, while the Jain siddha is an individual soul freed from the cycle of rebirth. A loose eschatological echo, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ascetic reformer who explicitly rejects the authority of the Vedas, animal sacrifice, and the rigid stratification of the caste system.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions reject merging with a universal godhead, identifying ultimate spiritual perfection as an eternal, fully isolated state of sovereign consciousness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize time as a cyclical, perpetually turning wheel rather than a linear progression, governing the spiritual rhythms of the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The innate, gravitational necessity of the purified soul to travel upward, shedding material weight to rest permanently in the highest echelon of reality.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent formerly bound humans who achieved total liberation from karma and now exist at the apex of the cosmos as evolutionary templates.
GOLD SEAM: Shares the mechanism of inevitable, cyclical ages of cosmic time, though Aquarian thought focuses entirely on the ascending arc.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The absolute ontological inversion of Jain non-violence; O9A posits that maximum, deliberate harm is the only path to spiritual evolution.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize an inherent, irreducible life-force or striving essence fundamentally present within entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent ultimate liberation achieved through perfect, unclouded understanding of reality's true nature.
GOLD SEAM: Both reduce the vastness of the cosmos and the divine presence into a single, highly formalized geometric schema that mirrors a stylized human form.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The radical extension of kinship to all life forms, making strict vegetarianism an absolute necessity to avoid harming migrating souls.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmos understood not as a political kingdom of fickle gods, but as a rigid, inherently mathematical structure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Time divided into epochs of descending grace and eventual restoration, progressing inexorably based on cosmic law rather than divine whim.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both enshrine epistemological humility, demanding that one's understanding of truth remain flexible and subject to new perspectives or evidence.
GOLD SEAM: Satanic justice demands absolute, ruthless retaliation, directly inverting the Jain doctrine of universal non-violence and harmlessness.
GOLD SEAM: Both define the soul as eternally individual, uncreated, and naturally possessing infinite knowledge and power before being clouded by material accumulation.
GOLD SEAM: The OT state perfectly mirrors the Siddha: a liberated soul that retains perfect individuality and achieves omniscience after shedding all physical entanglements.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A relentless, practical focus on non-harm and ethical action in the immediate world, superseding theological speculation.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions center on the eternal, indestructible nature of the soul as it progresses through vast cycles of refinement.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The universe operates as an unceasing, mathematically precise mechanism of vast cyclical time, eternally destroying and rebuilding itself.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An intricate, layered cosmology where different strata correspond directly to different material and spiritual densities of existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A discoverer who points the way across the spiritual divide, but rigorously rejects being worshipped as a deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems feature heavily structured, multi-tiered cosmologies where a soul's placement is perfectly, mechanically dictated by its internal spiritual weight.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions locate the true essence of the individual not in outward bodily form but in an irreducible, eternal inner substance that shapes its own spiritual destiny.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions view the ultimate spiritual culmination not as merging with the universe, but as an eternal, autonomous, and perfected isolate state.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions view spiritual impediment as a literal, material 'dust' that accumulates on and obscures the inherent purity of the soul.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions envision the ultimate spiritual state as a permanent, individualized, and autonomous consciousness, rather than merging back into a universal source.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions posit an eternal, indestructible unit of consciousness navigating an immense cycle of physical incarnations.
GOLD SEAM: The gradual descent into dense matter followed by an ascent toward spiritualization mirrors the Jain cosmic wheel of time.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize the irreducible, pure consciousness as the fundamental, immortal nature of existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a discrete, indestructible locus of individual consciousness that can migrate between forms without losing its fundamental nature.
GOLD SEAM: Both describe the ultimate spiritual destination as a literal, physical zenith in the cosmos where liberated, deathless beings reside.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The structural codification of epistemic humility, acknowledging that infinite truth cannot possibly be captured by a single dogma or perspective.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An indestructible, pure inner core of being that must labor to overcome material entanglements to achieve ultimate cosmic liberation.
GOLD SEAM: Both establish an absolute, foundational injunction against causing harm to other living beings, though operating in vastly different cosmic frameworks.
GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize time not as a linear march to apocalypse, but as a perpetual, spinning wheel of cyclical return.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The eternal soul traveling through material forms to shed impurities and accrue consequences, though Yarsanism limits this process strictly to 1,001 lifetimes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Infinite, eternal souls whose pure nature is obscured by the karmic weight of the physical universe.

dossier.essence: An ancient Indian religion of radical non-violence and asceticism, traced through a lineage of twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras ('ford-makers'), of whom the last two — Pārśvanātha (traditionally c. 8th c. BCE) and Mahāvīra (traditionally c. 599–527 BCE, a contemporary of the Buddha) — are historically attested. Held today by several million adherents, mainly in India, it teaches that every living thing houses an eternal soul (jīva) bound by karmic matter, and that liberation comes through non-harm and the shedding of karma. Distinctively, it is a religion with no creator god.
dossier.cosmos: The universe (loka), shaped like a vast cosmic man, is eternal, uncreated, and self-existent — there is no maker at its center, only infinite souls, matter, and impersonal law. Time turns as a great wheel (kāla-cakra) of descending and ascending ages, worsening and mending by its own motion without any divine judgment. Souls transmigrate through countless rebirths driven by karma conceived as a subtle physical substance; the liberated soul (siddha) rises to a crescent at the summit of the cosmos and abides forever — omniscient, bodiless, and eternally distinct, never merged into any absolute.
dossier.practice: The governing vow is ahiṃsā, non-injury to any being down to the one-sensed lives of earth, water, fire, and air — upheld by restraint rather than sacrifice. Mendicant monks and nuns take the five great vows (mahāvratas), sweep their path, filter their water, and may undertake fasting unto death (sallekhanā); the laity keep lesser vows and avoid the harming trades. The two main orders are the Digambara ('sky-clad') and Śvetāmbara ('white-clad'); epistemic humility is itself doctrine, in anekāntavāda, the many-sidedness of truth.
dossier.canon: The Śvetāmbara Āgamas (including the Ācārāṅga and Kalpa Sūtras) and, across both sects, Umāsvāti's 'Tattvārtha Sūtra' (c. 2nd–5th c. CE); the Digambaras hold the ancient canon lost and rely on later authoritative works (e.g. those of Kundakunda). The Tīrthaṅkaras are enumerated in the Kalpa Sūtra.
dossier.reflects: A cosmos that needs no god — decline and renewal, bondage and liberation all run by impersonal law, and salvation is an attainment repeated by countless equal souls rather than a gift of grace or a merger into the One.
sources: Padmanabh S. Jaini, 'The Jaina Path of Purification' (1979)
Kristi L. Wiley, 'Historical Dictionary of Jainism' (2004)
Nathmal Tatia (trans.), 'That Which Is: Tattvārtha Sūtra' (1994)
